Dear Jeanne:
sometime aCJo, we had a telephone conversation regarding
disclosure responsibil ities of affiliated entities. 
Below is
question srhich I posed to you and the anslrer as I understood
ouestion
When a llajor Donor filer 
(FPPC Forlu 46L) and a Recipient
coDmittee filer 
(FPPC Forn 420) are affiliated 
for Iinitation
purposes, as that term is used in FPPC ReguLation L8531.5 (a) and
(c), nust the campaign disclosure reports reflect conbined totals?
Ansser
we agreed that the onl1r relevant stat-ute or regn:lation
in chapter 4 of the Political 
Reform Act on affiliated 
campaign
disclosure was f'PPc Regulation La42a. This regulation reguires a
consolidated report of affiliated 
entities which do 4q! receive
contributions. This regmlation is most commonly used to aggreqate
contributions of parent and subsidiary corporations on a single
Major Donor campaign statement. We both agTreed that there was no
IegaJ. reguirement to disclose or combine the activity of a uajor
Donor filer 
and a Recipient Committee filer on the same campaiqn
statement. You did suggest, however, that rnajor donor filers 
and
recipient 
committees vrhich do aggregate contributions 
for
linitation 
purposes make a footnote reference on both reports to
the fact that both entities monitor campaign contributions and do
not colLectively exceed the linits 
provided in chapter 5 of the
Political Refom Act.


l{s. Jeanne Pritchard
Page 2

conversation on the subject. If
not hesitate to call ne,

Re: Request 8or Aalvice
Dear Kathy:
This office serves as 1egal counsel and I serve as
treasurer to a federal nonconnected committee, San Franciscans
Getting Things Done, I.D. No. COO229O39. This Committee was
established on June 20, 1988. The Cornmittee was established to
permit San Francisco Mayor Art Agnos to raise and disburse funds
in support of candidates for federal office. 
He currently serves
as Chair of the Cornmittee. For two years now this coromittee has
been raising and spending noney to influence federal elections.
In calendar year 1990 and for the first time, I have been
requested to issue several contribution checks (each for less than
91,000) to candidates for California state and local office from
this account. The total of these checks will be more than gL,OOO
but significantly 
less than $1O,OOO. This firm has reviewed
federal canpaiqn law and has determined that such disbursernents
from this account are permissible under federal 1avr. However, I
seek advice in perrnitting such disbursements from the committee in
light of the involvernent of 
Mayor Agnos, an officeholder and
candidate as defined by the political Reforn Act.
. 
In trying to determine what lar,r applies to this
conmittee, I looked at past Commission advice.
First, 
you provided informal advice to the Federal
Election Commission regarding a request it 
received fron
Congressman Don Edlrards regarding his California 
reporting
responsibil ities if his conmittee contributed to californii 
state
and locaI candidates (Fppc Advice Letter r-89-687). rn that letter
you indicated, in part, that congressman Edwards r federai carnpaign
cornmittee would not becone a rrcomrnittee, under catifornia law untir


Kathryn Donovan, Esq.
Page 2
it contributed $10,000 or more to California state andlor local
candidates. 
In that event, it would become a itmajor donor
committeerr as defined in Government Code 82013 (c). 
That advice
Ietter went on to indicate that it rras the Comrnission staffrs
opinion that a federal principal, canpaiqn committee would never
become a rrrecipient comnitteerr pursuant to California Government
Code section 8201,3 (a) .
Second, as 
I 
understand it 
the 
Connissionrs
interpretation of the definition of contribution contained in
Regulation 1,8215 (c) is that a federal separate segregated fund
(PAC) or a nonconnected federal committee is viewed identically to
a federal principal campaign comroittee in its first calendar year
in the eyes of the Political Refonn Act (PRA), but such a cornmittee
(PRA) would become a trrecipient connittee under the PRAtt if, in a
second or subsequent year, the federal corornittee contributed $l-,000
or more to california state and/or local candidates because its
donors 
hrould have rrreason to knowrr sone of their 
federal
contribution was being used for California state and local
purposes. Such a federal corornittee that contributes $1,000 or
nore in a subsequent calendar year would then be reguired to
register with the Secretary of State and file 
Form 420 reports
disclosing not only the California disbursements/contributions to
candidates but a pro rata portion of all contributions received
by the committee that funded these disbursements. As a "recipient
committeerr under the PRA it would be subject to the transfer ban
in Government Code Section 85304 and Irlayor Agnos would be a
controlling candidate and thus the committee could nake no
contributions to state or local candidates.
As mentioned above, this is the first year our committee
has considered making any contributions to California candidates.
As stated, the sum of these contributions is weII below $l"O,OOO.
Mayor Art Agnos has not contributed to the committee. we agree
that l,Iayor Art Agnos is a candidate as defined in Government Code
S82007, however, this federal cornrnittee will 
not become a
trconmitteerr as defined in Government Code Section 82013 in 1990
since:a) 
no contributors have made contributions to us to support
California candidates;
b) 
we will not be rnaking any independent expenditures; and,
c) 
we will not make contributions to California candidates
totaling $10,OOO or nore in a calendar year.


Kathryn Donovan, Esq.
Page 3
The prohibition on transfers contained in Governrnent Code
S85304 states:
llo csrdidate for etective office or ccrrni ttee coritrolled
by th6t ca.didote or c..rdidrt6 for clectiyc officr Eh6tt
!-t!ELgf a,ry co.ltributio.r to any other candid.tc fot ctective
office. Transfers of turds b.tleen c6rdid6tes or their controtled
coimi ttees are prohibitcd.(E[ph6sis added)
trcontrolled coumitteerr as used in Governnent Code 585304 also is
specifically defined in the -4ct at Government Ccde 582016.
It appears that San Franciscans cetting Things Done wilL
not be a rrcommitteerr in 1.990 and therefore v/i11 not be a
rrcontrolled comrnitteerr under the PRA. Consequently it appears to
ne that this federal conrmittee nay contribute to California state
and local candidates without violating 
the transfer ban in
Governnent Code section 85304 if 
total 
PRA contributions
(candidates and ballot neasures) remain below $10,000 in 1,990 and
below $L,000 in any subseguent year.
Please advise us at your earliest convenience regarding
your interpretation of this issue.
Things
Done
